author: @NorthernKitty
text: Definitely worthy of the adjective "AWESOME!"  😋  I've found this stuff VERY handy to keep around for cutting custom pieces to cover gaps - for example, when your air-conditioner doesn't quite fit in your window, or even covering a broken window until you get a chance to fix it.  Instead of using a washer on your balancing arm, I'd use a row of staples wide enough to fit over the arm (or similarly bent metal) - that way, you can slide it back and forth without it falling off!

author: @IrrationalRecreation
text: There's a brand called Eco Core by Homeline which has an inner core made from recycled uPVC doors and windows.  I have some left over from a building construction project and it is very useful material.  One caution is that the fascia board has a very slightly curved profile or at least the ones I have do.

author: @spex357
text:
For those who have never cut a foam board with a knife the key word was score. It is best done against an edge. Be aware that foam board is slippy and freehand scoring is dangerous as you are drawing the knife towards you and there is friction against the blade, meaning you are exerting quite a lot of force, one slip...and I wouldn't recommend it, at all. 
A fine toothed hand saw as mentioned by Robert leaves little waste, and is effortless to use.

(mm perfect) although i do ave 5mm rubbers that i have the 'moving parts' hit before the 'shell' it gives me a look into the 'engine' or 'peripheral' i build.
i basically need to look into the 'machine' while programming the code, so when the user buys it, they cannot break it lol...

author: @K9JT
text:
Thank you rob for being so inspirational and influential in bringing innovation and creativity down to earth for the average backyard enthusiast. The world needs more encouragement on this front for humans to not "evolve" into mindless drones.
I've noticed it's too easy for us to latch onto the latest and greatest tech and forget how to use "simpler", more commonly available and undoubtedly cheaper alternatives.
We get trapped into the perception of thinking that innovation can only happen in the spaces where money and media interest gets diverted, and those without access or means to play in that space get very discouraged and give up on their ideas and society loses out on potential that will never be realised.
I'm speaking from experience and also analytical observation of what's going on in the world so this isn't just a fanboy comment because you put out another cool video. It's more of a sincere appreciation for the content you put out and the way in which you share your knowledge.
Anyway, I hope this was of interest to you. Thank you very much for reading :-)

author: @jimmacaulay844
text:
Nice!  What we have readily in the US is called foam board, ¼ or ½" thick, with paper backing.  It's very flat, the paper seems pretty rigid, and the white variety is brilliant white.  It's used as a backer board for artwork (though I'm not certain it's acid-free), model making, and it's great as a cheap reflector for photography. And it weighs pretty close to zero.

Might not be quite as strong as your builder board, but it gets massive points for price and availability - every hobby shop.

author: @brilliantgamestudios
text: Correct me if I'm wrong, and this is off topic.  But I've noticed you haven't ever made a Lead Oxide Zinc battery using alum as an electrolyte.  I have had a ton of success with this battery, and I'm only charging the lead as is, with paper towel wrapped around it to hold the lead oxide.  I've gotten a small pill bottle size cell to reach 100mah at 2.5 volts.  The amperage reached peaks of 3-4 amps!  I made 6 of them and was able to briefly turn on an inverter and house lamp!  Once I get one working properly it's invincible and actually gets stronger each cycle!  BTW, thanks for your videos.  I've been learning a lot from you!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I have mate - and I agree with you they are awesome

replies.author: @philipvernejules9926
replies.text: .....that'd be one where he shows improvements to a lead acid battery after dismantling. A video around a year ago . lead anode is removed , replaced with carbon felt if i remember , plated with zinc  from zinc sulphate in electrolyte solution to form new anode .Copper was the other choice if I can remember. Zinc does have a bit of an issue with formation of dendrite fingers . The prolific videos are almost a confused blur now already , hundreds of them. . . . . .I imagine you have a lead plate as starting point for cathode which forms lead dioxide upon first  charge . it'd probably become more porous with each charge /discharge cycle to improve performance and alum as electrolyte would prevent deterioration of the paper wrap around cathode compared to acid but I wonder .

replies.author: @brilliantgamestudios
replies.text: ​@philipvernejules9926 Yeah I'm just smashing a thick lead wire(Used for fishing weight) into long plates and rolling them up into a cell.  And yeah, alum is really stable and pretty cheap.  It's often hard to get a good oxide layer to form using this method.  I find leaving the plates out for several days helps alot.   But once it starts working, it really works.

replies.author: @brilliantgamestudios
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Awesome, I'll have to watch the video Philip mentioned.  Would you consider doing this from scratch with raw lead and alum? Instead of acid and pre pasted plates?  If you shop properly, its all stuff that is super cheap and easy to get a hold of.  Perfect DIY video.

author: @Scott_C
text: In America, as far as I know, this stuff is hard to find. A product called foam core is often easy to find however it is not PVC Sandwiching foam it is Paper Sandwiching foam. I've been googling to find the PVC foam core but it's not as easy to find in a retail shop as you'd think.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is a shame mate - it is awesome stuff and all over the place here - I did just check eBay.com and it seems most uk companies will ship

replies.author: @Aethalops
replies.text: It's not as commonly used in fascia products (here in the USA), but if you just search UPVC Boards, the big box stores have a variety of products made from this stuff. I haven't concluded whether it's actually inexpensive compared to other options for a given project.

replies.author: @Scott_C
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering need a direct link. Ebay US doesn't think it exists.

replies.author: @Scott_C
replies.text: It's funny too because in the US the wording and language is tricky. The foam in the middle is often called Expanded PVC, even if it's not the right term. So searching for PVC foam board often returns an un-sandwiched board of just foam, which is of no use.  And if you search "foam core" all it gives you is the paper stuff. Also the online retailers don't do us or the product any help because they never specifically describe the product. They talk about the thickness and they say foam is on the inside but they don't say what the outer layers are made of because the just refer to it as "PVC foam". Frustrating.

replies.author: @Scott_C
replies.text: @Aethalops  yeah no luck mate. Just did a search of  Ebay, Amazon, Lowes, HD, for UPVC board. When you search UPVC, at best, you get the stuff that is just sheets of hard plastic, stuff that needs a circular/hand saw to cut. No foam in the middle.

replies.author: @tombrooks2015
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering all joking aside, I've always imagined a barrel full of old air conditioning compressors ( they are so heavy and I run a air conditioning business) sunk into the ground attached to a winch/pulley system, using wind/solar/manual energy to lift it and when power is required it falls to operate a generator, even capturing the energy from rainfall through a gutter system to power a battery that generates lift on the barrel when it's full.
Just lift the barrel.
How heavy would the barrel need to be to generate plenty of power for a standard size household.
There you go, a new project free of charge and I will be tuned in for every second 👍
